questions.question: What does 'type erasure' mean for Java generics at runtime?
choices: Type arguments are kept by the JVM and recoverable via reflection.
The type argument is checked by javac and then removed; at runtime the JVM sees raw types.
Generics are translated into a single shared template at runtime.
The JIT specialises code per type argument, like C#.
questions.explanation: Java erases generic type arguments after compile-time checking. The bytecode operates on raw types (List, Map), which is the price the language paid for backward compatibility when generics were added in Java 5.

questions.question: You need to deserialize JSON into List<Order> using Jackson. Why is mapper.readValue(json, List.class) wrong, and what is the right pattern?
choices: List.class is wrong; pass List<Order>.class instead.
List.class loses the Order type due to erasure; use new TypeReference<List<Order>>() {} so the anonymous subclass captures the parameterised type.
Use Class.forName("List<Order>") to obtain a reified type token.
Both forms are equivalent; pick either.
questions.explanation: Class literals can't be parameterised - List.class is just the raw List class. The anonymous-subclass trick (new TypeReference<List<Order>>() {}) captures the full parameterised type in the subclass's metadata, which survives erasure.

questions.question: Which signature correctly applies PECS for a method that copies from a source to a destination?
choices: copy(List<? extends Number> src, List<? super Number> dst)
copy(List<? super Number> src, List<? extends Number> dst)
copy(List<Number> src, List<? extends Number> dst)
questions.explanation: PECS: Producer Extends, Consumer Super. The source produces Numbers (read-only) so it's ? extends Number; the destination consumes Numbers (write-only) so it's ? super Number. This lets the caller pass List<Integer> as src and List<Object> as dst.
